metrics.narrative: No quantified business case, savings target, or ROI articulated by the buyer across six touchpoints spanning 12 Mar to 29 Jun 2026. David Elford stated 6 Apr the driver is 'change management, 2,000 people used to doing it a certain way' and 'the issue is change management… we've got 2,000 people who are used to doing this a certain way that we're going to have to convince that we're going now do it a different way.' This frames the pain as operational disruption risk, not a quantified cost or efficiency problem. No dollar savings, headcount reduction, cycle-time improvement, or error-rate metric named. The buyer's CFO challenged procurement to 'evaluate market alternatives beyond the current suite' (30 Mar demo, background note), but no financial threshold, budget envelope, or payback period was stated. Pricing was well received 29 Jun (Next Step: 'Pricing well received'), but no CFO or budget owner validated the $500k implementation investment vs. a Coupa renewal with zero implementation cost. Without a quantified value case anchored to business outcomes, the deal is vulnerable to status-quo inertia: Coupa can offer a renewal with no change management, no disruption, and no upfront cost, making it the path of least resistance. The gap is a knowledge gap: no buyer quantification exists across calls, Next Steps, tasks, or fields. Closing the gap requires co-building an ROI model with David, Whitney, and the AP team that translates 'change management' pain into measurable operational savings (e.g. AP cycle time, manual PO-invoice matching hours, procurement headcount efficiency) and presenting it to the CFO/CPO this week to justify the switch.

competition.narrative: Bass Pro is running a two-vendor evaluation: Zycus (challenger) vs. Coupa renewal (incumbent). Coupa is the sole named competitor and holds the defensive advantage with 2,000 entrenched downstream P2P users, zero implementation cost, and a Feb 2027 renewal window that allows them to retain without forcing a switch decision. Coupa's competitive position escalated significantly on 29 Jun when they scheduled an onsite visit for 3 Jul 2026 (Next Step 29 Jun: 'RISK- coupa is going onsite wednesday'), a late-stage retention play timed exactly when Bass Pro must decide (decision window 6-13 Jul per Next Step 29 Jun: 'they need to make a decision in the coming week or so'). This is a head-to-head bakeoff at the moment of truth. Coupa's strengths: (1) Incumbent with 6 years of history at Bass Pro. David stated 12 Mar (intro call transcript) 'we've actually been with Coupa for 6 now' and 'we like to… kick the tires and make sure that we are using the best tool that we can and getting the best deal we can every 3 years or so.' (2) Embedded workflows and user familiarity. David stated 6 Apr 'we've got 2,000 people who are used to doing this a certain way that we're going to have to convince that we're going now do it a different way.' Coupa can offer continuity with no change management, training, or disruption. (3) Zero implementation cost on renewal. Zycus requires $500k+ implementation (1.1-1.3x software per 6 Apr proposal) and 6-18 months to go live. Coupa can renew in 30 days with no services cost. (4) Onsite escalation this week. Coupa is bringing their A-team onsite 3 Jul to counter Zycus's pricing and positioning, likely offering pricing concessions, product roadmap commitments, or executive sponsorship. Coupa's weaknesses: (1) Poor spend analytics and search UX. Whitney stated 12 Mar 'Coupa spend analysis: new platform is cumbersome; users revert to ad hoc reporting' and 'they sunset it and moved it into a new module, and the new module is just… it's not the same.' 19 Mar technical discovery pain points: 'Poor search and catalog prioritization leading users to non-preferred vendors.' (2) Slow support response times. David stated 12 Mar 'support for them has been kind of a pain point for us as well' and 'response to those tickets has been… it's not great.' Whitney confirmed 'ticket support response times from Coupa are slow, impacting error resolution' (12 Mar challenges note). (3) No native intake management. David confirmed 12 Mar 'that's functionality we don't have today is a single intake for all the different types of buying… that would be a big improvement.' Coupa lacks Merlin Intake and guided buying. Zycus's wedge is Microsoft Teams-native intake + Merlin AI orchestration + unified S2P with better analytics and search, but the wedge has not been converted into a quantified ROI or exec-validated business case. Without EB engagement and references completed, Coupa's 'no change, no risk, no cost' renewal pitch is the path of least resistance. Coupa's threat level is HIGH and rising: the onsite visit 3 Jul is a live retention block, and Zycus has 6 days (until 13 Jul decision window closes) to secure references, book the exec alignment meeting, and counter Coupa's pitch. If Coupa shifts the buyer's timeline or secures CFO/CPO commitment onsite, the deal flips or slips past 30 Jul close. Status: Coupa is active and escalating. No other competitors named.

identify_pain.narrative: Bass Pro's procurement and AP teams are experiencing three operational pains driving this evaluation, all articulated by David Elford (Director, Non-Merchandise Procurement) and Whitney Parks (Non Merchandise Procurement Manager) across calls from 12 Mar to 29 Jun 2026. The pain owner is David Elford, who is the project lead and primary contact. Pain 1: Fragmented intake and lack of request control. David stated 30 Mar (demo background note) the driver is 'implement intake system to replace tribal knowledge and ensure compliant sourcing requests.' The 19 Mar technical discovery identified 'fragmented intake and inconsistent buying entry points' as a current-state pain. Bass Pro has no centralized intake funnel; requests come in via email, phone, and ad hoc channels, creating compliance risk and zero visibility for procurement. David said 12 Mar (intro call transcript) 'that's functionality we don't have today is a single intake for all the different types of buying. That's nice to have. We don't have it today, but that would be a big improvement.' This pain is operational (manual, invisible, non-compliant requests) and confirmed across multiple calls. Pain 2: Coupa's cumbersome spend analytics and poor search/catalog prioritization. Whitney stated 12 Mar (intro call notes) 'Coupa spend analysis: new platform is cumbersome; users revert to ad hoc reporting' and 'the real shame is they used to have a great tool… 2020 to 2023 or '4 was really good, and then they sunset it and moved it into a new module, and the new module is just… it's not the same.' The 19 Mar technical discovery pain points list: 'Poor search and catalog prioritization leading users to non-preferred vendors' and 'Spend analytics driven by UNSPSC and manual reclassification, losing original commodity context.' Bass Pro's category managers are frustrated by Coupa's 300-menu analytics UI and inability to self-serve; procurement must run reports for them. The search weakness drives users to non-preferred suppliers, eroding savings and compliance. This pain is operational (manual reporting, lost savings, user frustration) and is owned by Whitney and the category manager team (Brent Kluksdahl, Jennifer Gosney on OpportunityContactRole). Pain 3: Change management and user-adoption risk for 2,000 downstream P2P users. David stated 6 Apr (proposal review call) 'the biggest issue with this is… change management, and we've got 2,000 people who are used to doing this a certain way that we're going to have to convince that we're going now do it a different way, and that's the hard part.' This is a forward-looking pain: switching from Coupa to any new platform creates disruption risk, training burden, and potential user resistance. David confirmed 'all we can do to assist with that change management is gonna have to happen' (6 Apr). This pain is organizational (adoption risk, training cost, disruption) and is owned by David and the AP team (who David said 6 Apr 'know they're nervous about considering anything different'). All three pains are confirmed and articulated by named buyers; none are inferred. The pains are operational (manual work, user frustration, compliance risk, adoption risk) rather than financial, which is why no quantified savings or ROI has been stated — the business case is qualitative improvement, not dollar savings. This explains the metrics gap and creates the vulnerability that Coupa can exploit: if the pain is 'change is hard,' Coupa's renewal pitch is 'no change, no risk, no cost.'

Bass Pro highlighted Coupa's weakness: 'poor search and catalog prioritization leading users to non-preferred vendors' (19 Mar pain point). Zycus must prove configurable search ranking and custom metadata to pass this filter. (3) T&E integration preserving Egencia: 'improved travel and expense integration was emphasized as critical while preserving existing functionalities like eGencia' (27 May T&E demo key takeaway). The buyer uses Egencia for travel booking and Coupa for expense reporting; the new platform must integrate both without disruption. (4) Unified spend analytics with AI-driven taxonomy: 'unified taxonomy for P2P, expense, and capex spend analysis with AI classification' (19 Mar technical discovery need). Bass Pro's pain is 'Coupa spend analysis: new platform is cumbersome; users revert to ad hoc reporting' (19 Mar competitor note) and 'spend analytics driven by UNSPSC and manual reclassification, losing original commodity context' (19 Mar pain point). Zycus must prove AI auto-classification and single-taxonomy reporting across P2P + T&E + capex to win this criterion. (5) Change management and user adoption for 2,000 downstream users: David stated 6 Apr 'the biggest issue with this is… change management, and we've got 2,000 people who are used to doing this a certain way that we're going to have to convince that we're going now do it a different way.' Zycus's Microsoft Teams integration ('eliminates need to learn new software, increasing user adoption' — 6 Apr key takeaway) and offer of 'workshops, lunch-and-learns, and on-site open office sessions' (6 Apr change management approach) directly address this criterion. (6) Reference validation from Coupa-to-Zycus P2P customers: David requested 6 Apr 'two or three references… P2P customers who were previous Coupa customers… to hear their thoughts on the transition, how their organization adapted, and what change management was involved.' This is a formal gate in the decision process — no selection without reference calls. (7) 5-year pricing with transparent module breakdown and implementation cost amortization: David requested 6 Apr '5 year price' and 'a little more detail on what is included in those modules' (iSource, iContract, iSupplier one-pagers). He confirmed 'if we were gonna undertake this and incur all the implementation expense, I think we'd wanna definitely plan on staying put for 5 years.' Zycus must provide transparent 5-year SaaS + implementation pricing with module-by-module breakdown to pass commercial gate. All seven criteria are confirmed across calls and Next Steps; Zycus has addressed 1-5 and 7 (pricing delivered and well received 29 Jun), but criterion 6 (references) remains open and is blocking final decision.

ai_fit_signal.summary: No formal AIS (AI Score) field populated in Salesforce for this deal. Inferring AI appetite from buyer behavior and articulated needs: Bass Pro is AI-aware and AI-interested but not AI-hungry. They are evaluating Zycus's Merlin Intake + AI-driven spend analytics as solutions to operational pains (fragmented intake, cumbersome Coupa analytics) rather than pursuing AI as a strategic imperative. David confirmed 12 Mar Merlin Intake 'would be a big improvement' and Whitney articulated 12 Mar + 19 Mar Coupa's analytics weakness, showing they value AI-driven automation and intelligence when it solves a known pain. However, no buyer statement frames AI as a strategic priority, competitive differentiator, or C-level mandate. The CFO's challenge to 'evaluate market alternatives' (30 Mar demo background) was not AI-driven; it was cost/value-driven. Tier: AI Curious. The buyer is open to AI capabilities that directly address their pains and will adopt them if the ROI is clear, but AI is not the driver of the deal. Calls do not show AI under-rated (buyer appetite is genuine and aligned with Zycus's positioning) or over-rated (no inflated AI expectations). AI is appropriately positioned as an enabler of intake orchestration and analytics intelligence, not a standalone value prop.

Pricing was well received 29 Jun, but no CFO/budget owner validated the $500k implementation investment. Business case is qualitative (better intake, better analytics, better UX) but not quantified. Gap: no dollar-anchored ROI to justify the switch vs. Coupa's zero-cost renewal.

David's championship is genuine, continuous, and directive (he is running the process, not just participating), but it is incomplete: he is not the budget owner and has not yet escalated to the CFO/CPO to secure EB sponsorship. His strength is developing-to-strong with one critical gap: without EB engagement, his advocacy cannot convert to a signed contract. He is at risk of being overruled by Coupa's onsite pitch 3 Jul if the CFO/CPO is not aligned with Zycus before that meeting. Champion strength: strong but execution-incomplete.

items.action: Co-build quantified ROI model with David, Whitney, and AP team translating qualitative pains into measurable operational savings and present to CFO or CPO by 13 Jul
items.trigger: Pricing was well received 29 Jun (Next Step 29 Jun: 'Pricing well received'), but no quantified business case exists to justify the $500k implementation vs. Coupa renewal. items.expected_effect: A quantified ROI model anchored to business outcomes (e.g. AP cycle time reduction, procurement headcount efficiency, user productivity hours saved, error rate improvement) converts qualitative pain ('fragmented intake,' 'cumbersome analytics,' 'slow support') into a dollar-justified business case the CFO/CPO can approve. Presenting the ROI model in the exec alignment meeting (rank-2 move) gives the EB a financial anchor to champion the Zycus switch internally and counter Coupa's renewal pricing concessions. Without a quantified business case, the EB cannot justify the $500k implementation, and the deal stalls or flips to Coupa.

items.action: Formalize change-management plan with user adoption timeline, training schedule, and exec sponsorship commitment and present to David and AP team by 13 Jul

Zycus offered workshops, lunch-and-learns, and 24x5 service desk (6 Apr), and Microsoft Teams integration reduces learning curve, but no detailed change-management plan, user-adoption timeline, or exec-sponsored rollout roadmap has been formalized. David confirmed 6 Apr 'all we can do to assist with that change management is gonna have to happen,' but the plan is conceptual, not committed. David also noted 6 Apr the AP team 'know they're nervous about considering anything different.'
items.expected_effect: A formalized change-management plan with phased user rollout, training schedule, Teams-integration onboarding, and CFO/CPO exec sponsorship commitment de-risks David's single biggest concern ('the hard part' of convincing 2,000 users) and gives the AP team confidence the transition will be supported. Without a formal plan, the buyer may choose Coupa renewal to avoid the adoption risk. The change-management plan converts 'we can support you' into 'here is how we will support you, week by week,' making the switch decision less risky and more executable.

competitive_position.summary: Bass Pro is running a two-vendor evaluation (Zycus vs. Coupa renewal) with Coupa holding the defensive advantage as the 6-year incumbent with 2,000 entrenched P2P users, zero implementation cost, and a Feb 2027 renewal window that allows retention without forcing a switch. Coupa escalated competitive pressure 29 Jun by scheduling an onsite visit for 3 Jul 2026 — a late-stage retention play timed exactly when Bass Pro must decide (decision window 6-13 Jul). This is a head-to-head bakeoff at the moment of truth. Coupa's wedge is 'no change, no risk, no cost' — they can offer continuity with familiar workflows, no training burden, no implementation expense, and a 30-day renewal vs. Zycus's 6-18 month implementation. Zycus's wedge is Microsoft Teams-native Merlin Intake + unified S2P analytics + better search/catalog UX, directly addressing Bass Pro's stated pains (fragmented intake, cumbersome Coupa analytics, poor search). However, Zycus has not converted the wedge into a quantified ROI or secured CFO/CPO engagement, leaving the business case qualitative and vulnerable to Coupa's status-quo pitch. Coupa's weaknesses (poor analytics UX, slow support, no intake) are confirmed and articulated by Whitney Parks and David Elford across calls, but Coupa can counter by promising product roadmap improvements and pricing concessions onsite 3 Jul. The single strongest CURRENT threat is Coupa's onsite visit 3 Jul: if Coupa secures CFO/CPO commitment or shifts the timeline during that meeting, the deal flips to Coupa renewal or slips past 30 Jul close. Zycus has 6 days (until 13 Jul decision window closes) to complete reference calls, book the exec alignment meeting (Zycus RVP + Bass Pro CFO/CPO), and counter Coupa's pitch with a quantified business case and exec sponsorship. Without these moves this week, Coupa's 'no change' renewal is the path of least resistance. Threat level: HIGH and rising. Status: Coupa is active, escalating, and holds the inside track on retention. No other competitors in play.

ai_positioning_strength.summary: Zycus's AI positioning (Merlin Intake orchestrator + AI-driven spend analytics with auto-classification) is well-aligned with Bass Pro's stated needs and directly addresses their Coupa pains, but it has not been converted into a quantified business case or positioned as a strategic differentiator vs. Coupa's roadmap promises. Bass Pro articulated two AI-relevant pains: (1) 'fragmented intake and inconsistent buying entry points' (19 Mar technical discovery pain), which Merlin Intake solves by providing a single Teams-native request funnel with AI-validated questions and guided buying. (2) 'Coupa spend analysis: cumbersome; users revert to ad hoc reporting' and 'spend analytics driven by UNSPSC and manual reclassification, losing original commodity context' (12 Mar intro call + 19 Mar pain notes), which Zycus's AI auto-classification and unified taxonomy solve. Zycus demonstrated Merlin Intake 30 Mar ('Merlin Intake in Microsoft Teams provides a single request front door with configurable, AI-validated questions' — 30 Mar feature note) and AI spend analytics ('AI-driven spend analysis identified $1.3B consolidation opportunity among top 5 suppliers' — 30 Mar impact note), and David confirmed fit 12 Mar: 'that's functionality we don't have today… that would be a big improvement.' However, Zycus has not quantified the AI impact in Bass Pro terms (e.g. 'Merlin Intake reduces non-compliant requests by X%, saving procurement Y hours/week' or 'AI auto-classification eliminates manual UNSPSC rework, saving Z analyst hours/month'). Without a quantified AI value case, Coupa can promise AI roadmap improvements onsite 3 Jul and neutralize Zycus's positioning. AI positioning strength: under-positioned. Zycus has the capability and demonstrated it, but has not translated it into a dollar-anchored, exec-level strategic wedge that the CFO/CPO can champion. The move is to co-build an AI-driven ROI model with David and present it to the CFO/CPO this week, positioning Merlin + AI analytics as the operational-efficiency engine that justifies the $500k implementation vs. Coupa's manual, UNSPSC-driven status quo.

items.quote: David confirmed 12 Mar 'that's functionality we don't have today is a single intake for all the different types of buying. That's nice to have. We don't have it today, but that would be a big improvement.' Zycus demonstrated Merlin Intake in Microsoft Teams 30 Mar: 'Merlin Intake in Microsoft Teams provides a single request front door with configurable, AI-validated questions' (30 Mar demo feature note). David confirmed fit 30 Mar: 'Yeah. That sounds great. I would like to see that in the demo whenever you guys can set that up' (12 Mar intro call transcript).
items.source: Avoma 12 Mar 2026 intro call; Avoma 30 Mar 2026 demo
items.criterion: Downstream P2P with unified intake management

items.quote: 19 Mar technical discovery confirmed 'Customization flexibility is critical, including custom metadata fields, approval chain configurations, and guided buying with preferred punch-out catalogs prioritized in search' (key takeaway). David asked 30 Mar demo 'Do we have the ability to configure what order things are presented so the things that we truly want them to pick are shown first?' (question note). Zycus confirmed 30 Mar 'Guided buying supports punch-outs and catalog items in one interface with configurable order of presentation' (feature note). Fit confirmed.
items.source: Avoma 19 Mar 2026 technical discovery; Avoma 30 Mar 2026 demo
items.criterion: Customization flexibility (metadata fields, approval chains, catalog search prioritization)

items.quote: 27 May T&E demo confirmed 'Improved travel and expense integration was emphasized as critical while preserving existing functionalities like eGencia' (key takeaway) and 'Egencia was confirmed as the preferred T&E partner, with an emphasis on streamlined receipt uploads via mobile' (key takeaway). Zycus + Emburse integration demonstrated mobile receipt capture and Egencia booking integration. Fit confirmed.
items.source: Avoma 27 May 2026 T&E demo
items.criterion: T&E integration preserving Egencia with mobile receipt capture

items.quote: 19 Mar technical discovery need: 'Unified taxonomy for P2P, expense, and capex spend analysis with AI classification.' Bass Pro's pain is 'Spend analytics driven by UNSPSC and manual reclassification, losing original commodity context' (19 Mar pain point). 30 Mar demo confirmed Zycus AI-driven spend analysis with single taxonomy. David confirmed need 12 Mar: 'What would you like to get out of a spend analysis tool?' Answer: 'Something that's easy to use, easy to build your own views, easy to create… the data that you wanna see' (12 Mar intro call transcript). Fit confirmed.
items.source: Avoma 12 Mar 2026 intro call; Avoma 19 Mar 2026 technical discovery; Avoma 30 Mar 2026 demo
items.criterion: Unified AI-driven spend analytics with single taxonomy across P2P + T&E + capex

items.quote: 6 Apr proposal review delivered 5-year S2P pricing with full module breakdown (iSource, iContract, iSupplier, eProcurement, Merlin Intake, spend analytics, T&E). David requested 6 Apr '5 year price' and 'a little more detail on what is included in those modules.' Zycus delivered updated proposal, and 29 Jun Next Step confirms 'Pricing well received.' Fit confirmed.
items.source: Avoma 6 Apr 2026 proposal review; SF Next_Step__c 29 Jun 2026
items.criterion: Transparent 5-year pricing with module breakdown and implementation amortization

items.quote: David stated 6 Apr 'the biggest issue with this is… change management, and we've got 2,000 people who are used to doing this a certain way that we're going to have to convince that we're going now do it a different way, and that's the hard part.' Zycus offered 6 Apr 'workshops, lunch-and-learns, and on-site open office sessions to support stakeholder transition' (change management approach note) and positioned Microsoft Teams integration as reducing learning curve: 'Integration with Microsoft Teams eliminates need to learn new software, increasing user adoption' (6 Apr user adoption note). However, no detailed change-management plan, adoption timeline, or exec-sponsored rollout roadmap has been formalized. David confirmed 6 Apr 'all we can do to assist with that change management is gonna have to happen,' but the plan is conceptual, not committed. This is a partial fit: capability offered but execution confidence not established. Exposed.
items.source: Avoma 6 Apr 2026 proposal review
items.criterion: Change management support for 2,000 downstream P2P users

items.quote: David requested 6 Apr 'two or three references… P2P customers who were previous Coupa customers… to hear their thoughts on the transition, how their organization adapted, and what change management was involved.' This is a formal decision gate: no selection without reference calls. No evidence in Next Steps, tasks, or Avoma notes that references have been set up or completed as of 29 Jun. This is an exposed gap: buyer requested proof point to de-risk change-management concern, and Zycus has not delivered it. Without references, the buyer cannot validate the transition risk, and Coupa's 'no change' pitch wins. Exposed.
items.source: Avoma 6 Apr 2026 proposal review; no reference completion evidence in SF or Avoma
items.criterion: Reference validation from Coupa-to-Zycus P2P customers

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Bass Pro's stated evaluation criteria (downstream P2P with unified intake, customization flexibility, T&E integration preserving Egencia, unified AI-driven spend analytics, change management support, Coupa-to-Zycus reference validation, and transparent 5-year pricing with implementation amortization) are well-aligned with Zycus's full S2P platform offering, and pricing was well received 29 Jun. Zycus has demonstrated capability on five of seven criteria (P2P + intake, customization, T&E, analytics, pricing) across four product demos (12 Mar intro, 19 Mar technical discovery, 30 Mar full P2P demo, 27 May T&E demo) and delivered transparent 5-year commercial proposal 6 Apr. However, two critical criteria remain partially addressed or exposed: (1) Change management support for 2,000 downstream users is offered (workshops, lunch-and-learns, 24x5 service desk, Microsoft Teams integration to reduce learning curve per 6 Apr proposal) but not formalized into a detailed adoption roadmap or exec-sponsored change plan, leaving the buyer's single biggest concern (David 6 Apr: 'the hard part' is convincing 2,000 users to switch) unresolved. (2) Reference validation from Coupa-to-Zycus P2P customers (buyer requested 6 Apr) has not been completed, leaving the proof point that de-risks the transition undelivered. Without references and a formalized change-management plan with exec sponsorship, the fit is strong on product but exposed on execution confidence. Coupa can exploit this by offering 'no change, no risk' continuity, which directly counters Bass Pro's change-management anxiety. Zycus must close the reference and change-management gaps this week to convert product fit into decision confidence.

hard.ais_why:
• AI importance is logged as 'Neutral' and the agentic-platform discussion, Gen AI commentary and AI-RFP rating fields are all blank. The Merlin product line of Agentic AI, Merlin and Merlin Intake is Zycus-side tagging rather than a reflection of documented buyer asks.
• On the 89-minute March 30 commercial meeting with Whitney Parks, Kory Cooper and David Elford, the buyer's action items were strictly pricing-oriented: pricing templates, P2P invoice counts, contract volumes, T&E partner details, and module-based pricing options across P2P, P2P+Sourcing and P2P+Spin Analytics. AI, autonomous negotiation and Merlin did not surface once.
• All four captured meetings between March 12 and April 6 focus on P2P scoping, pricing and support model questions. With an explicit 'Neutral' AI posture and no buyer-voiced AI signal across nearly three hours of recorded discussion, this opportunity sits firmly in Resistant territory.
